scenario: A team of 5 modelers collaborates on a claim-processing BPMN in Web Modeler SaaS. Two designers open the same file simultaneously. One adds a Service Task; the other adds an Exclusive Gateway. They save within seconds of each other.
question: Which Web Modeler collaboration behavior applies?

options.text: md:231-234). Non-editors see updates and presence in real time, can switch modes, navigate the canvas, inspect properties, and add comments. A Take over button allows another editor to grab edit control (line 246). This is NOT Google-Docs-style simultaneous multi-cursor editing.

Web Modeler is strictly single-editor: when one user has edit permissions, the second user who opens the same file sees a prominent "file locked" full-screen message that blocks all access to the diagram, preventing any view, navigation, or comment activity until the lock is released. Web Modeler does lock the canvas while one user edits, but non-editors retain significant access: they can view the diagram in real time, navigate the canvas, inspect element properties, switch between viewer/editor mode, and add comments. A Take over button (not a full-screen block) allows handover.

Web Modeler uses optimistic concurrency (last-write-wins): if two users both have the file open in edit mode simultaneously, whichever user saves last silently overwrites the other user's changes, with no conflict detection or merge. Web Modeler prevents this scenario entirely through canvas locking. When one user with edit permissions starts editing, the canvas is locked automatically -- no second user can simultaneously acquire edit mode. The last-write-wins conflict scenario cannot occur because concurrent editing is structurally prevented.

Web Modeler uses a Git-style fork model: when a second user wants to make changes to a file that another user is editing, they must fork the file to create their own copy, make changes independently, and then submit a merge request for review before changes are incorporated. Web Modeler uses canvas locking with a Take over button -- not a fork model. The Take over action transfers edit control from the current editor to the second user directly, without creating separate file copies, merge requests, or any Git-style branching workflow.

a.text: Correct. Web Modeler does provide real-time view, presence indicators, and live updates of saved changes -- but the editing model is canvas-lock based, not Google-Docs concurrent. When one user with edit permissions starts editing, the canvas locks automatically and the second user sees a lock indicator; they can view, navigate, inspect, comment, and switch modes but cannot modify until the editor saves and releases (or until the second user clicks Take over to grab control). The scenario described (two designers concurrently adding elements and saving within seconds) cannot actually occur in Web Modeler -- the lock prevents simultaneous edits by design.

b.text: Incorrect. Web Modeler does lock the canvas while one user edits, but it does not show a generic "file locked" message blocking all access -- non-editors retain read/comment/navigate capabilities, and an explicit Take over action is available. Option (a) describes the real behavior more accurately.

c.text: Incorrect. Web Modeler does not use last-write-wins; the canvas lock prevents concurrent writes entirely.

d.text: Incorrect. No fork-required model; Take over is the canonical handover mechanism.

explanation: Web Modeler does provide real-time view, presence indicators, and live updates of saved changes -- but the editing model is canvas-lock based, not Google-Docs concurrent. When one user with edit permissions starts editing, the canvas locks automatically and the second user sees a lock indicator; they can view, navigate, inspect, comment, and switch modes but cannot modify until the editor saves and releases (or until the second user clicks Take over to grab control). The scenario described (two designers concurrently adding elements and saving within seconds) cannot actually occur in Web Modeler -- the lock prevents simultaneous edits by design.
